title: Hardcoded secret in JavaScript/TypeScript source code
summary: A password, API key, token, or other secret is hardcoded as a string literal in JavaScript or TypeScript source code. Hardcoded secrets are extracted trivially from source control history, build artifacts, bundled client-side code, and error messages — giving attackers persistent access to the associated service or account.
why_it_matters: Hardcoded secrets are one of the most common causes of credential exposure. Once a secret is committed to version control, it persists in the repository history even after deletion and can be found with automated scanning tools like trufflehog or gitleaks. In client-side JavaScript, hardcoded secrets are visible to anyone who inspects the browser's developer tools or the bundled source. Exposed API keys frequently lead to unauthorized access, data breaches, and financial loss — cloud provider keys alone have caused incidents costing hundreds of thousands of dollars in compute charges. Secrets also tend to be shared across environments, so a single leaked credential can compromise development, staging, and production systems simultaneously.
how_to_fix:
Move all secrets out of source code and into environment variables or a dedicated secrets manager.

1. Replace every hardcoded secret with a reference to process.env.VARIABLE_NAME.
2. Use a .env file with the dotenv package for local development — ensure .env is listed in .gitignore.
3. For production, inject secrets via your deployment platform's secrets management (AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, Azure Key Vault, Kubernetes Secrets, Vault by HashiCorp).
4. Rotate any secret that has been committed to source control — it must be considered compromised.
5. Add pre-commit hooks (e.g., detect-secrets, gitleaks) to prevent future secret commits.
6. For client-side applications (React, Next.js, Vue), use server-side API routes or a backend-for-frontend to keep secrets off the client entirely — NEXT_PUBLIC_ and REACT_APP_ prefixed variables are embedded in the bundle and visible to users.
developer_notes: In Node.js, process.env values are always strings — parse them as needed (parseInt, JSON.parse). For TypeScript, use a validation library like envalid or zod to parse and type environment variables at startup, which gives you type-safe access and clear error messages for missing configuration. Remember that NEXT_PUBLIC_ variables in Next.js and REACT_APP_ variables in Create React App are embedded into the client-side bundle at build time — they are not secret. Any value that must remain confidential should only be accessed in server-side code (API routes, getServerSideProps, server components).
verification_checklist: No API keys, tokens, passwords, or connection strings appear as string literals in source code
All secrets are loaded from environment variables or a secrets manager
.env files are listed in .gitignore and not committed to the repository
Previously committed secrets have been rotated and invalidated
Pre-commit hooks are configured to detect accidental secret commits
Client-side code does not reference secret values via NEXT_PUBLIC_ or REACT_APP_ prefixes

references.title: OWASP Top 10 A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures
